title: Dry, gritty or watering eyes
excerpt: Dry, gritty, or watering eyes are more common than ever. Ayurveda has named and understood this condition for centuries, and its daily remedies are simple enough for anyone.
body_html:
that familiar sting at the end of the day

You finish a long stretch at your desk or in front of a screen, blink, and feel it: a dry, scratchy sensation, as though sand has settled behind your eyelids. Or the opposite happens and your eyes water without warning, blurring your vision at the worst moment. Both experiences are far more common now than they were a generation ago, and most people simply rub their eyes and carry on.

Ayurveda has a name for the health of the eyes: netra swasthya, the wellbeing of the organ of sight. The classical texts treat the eyes as one of the most precious of the five sense organs, and they devote considerable attention to what disturbs them and how to restore them. Understanding the Ayurvedic view does not require you to abandon your eye drops. It simply gives you a framework for why your eyes feel the way they do, and what small daily habits can make a real difference.

Both experiences are far more common now than they were a generation ago, and most people simply rub their eyes and carry on.

the ayurvedic view: vata, pitta and the eye

According to the classical texts, the eyes are governed primarily by the fire element. The Sushruta Samhita, which contains the most detailed Ayurvedic account of eye diseases (netraroga), teaches that the organ of vision is the seat of tejas, the luminous quality associated with Pitta dosha (Sushruta Samhita, Uttaratantra). Alochaka Pitta, the sub-dosha that resides in the eyes, is responsible for the capacity to perceive light and form. When this sub-dosha is well nourished and balanced, the eyes are clear, moist, and comfortable.

Dryness and grittiness arise when Vata dosha, which is dry, light, and mobile by nature, increases in the body and specifically in the netra (eyes). Excessive screen exposure, late nights, grief, irregular eating, and exposure to dry wind and dust all aggravate Vata. The Ashtanga Hridaya, a compact classical text that synthesised earlier knowledge, notes that the eyes are particularly vulnerable to dryness when the body as a whole becomes depleted or when the person neglects sleep and rest (Ashtanga Hridaya, Uttarasthana).

Watering eyes can seem like the opposite problem, but they often share the same Vata root. When dryness irritates the delicate tissues of the conjunctiva, a reflex watering can occur. In some cases, excess Pitta, manifesting as heat and inflammation, also causes a watery, burning discharge. The Sushruta Samhita classifies dozens of distinct eye conditions with careful attention to which dosha is involved, which is a reminder that two people with 'watering eyes' may need quite different care (Sushruta Samhita, Uttaratantra).

gentle daily habits that support the eyes

The single most consistently recommended practice in the classical literature for eye health is triphala netra. Triphala, a combination of three fruits, is prepared as a mild decoction, cooled completely, strained through fine cloth until no particle remains, and then used to wash or bathe the eyes gently. The Ashtanga Hridaya recommends regular eye washing as part of the daily regimen, dinacharya, for maintaining healthy vision (Ashtanga Hridaya, Sutrasthana). You can use a small eye cup, tilt your head, and allow the cooled liquid to bathe each eye for a few seconds.

Rose water, made from Rosa damascena petals, has been used in Indian household practice for generations as a cooling application for tired or irritated eyes. It is cooling in effect, which makes it particularly suited for Pitta-related eye heat and redness. A single drop in each eye at the end of a working day is a simple ritual that many people find immediately soothing.

The classical texts also emphasise what to avoid. Sushruta Samhita warns against suppressing the urge to sleep, prolonged exposure to very bright light, crying without release, and staring fixedly without blinking, all of which deplete the moisture and vitality of the eyes (Sushruta Samhita, Uttaratantra). Applying oil to the soles of the feet before bed is a traditional Vata-pacifying practice that, according to the texts, benefits the entire nervous system and sense organs indirectly.

Diet also plays a role. Foods that are sweet, unctuous, and cooling, such as ghee, soaked almonds, ripe sweet fruits, and cooked leafy greens, support the nourishment of the eye tissues. The Ashtanga Hridaya places ghee among the substances most beneficial for the eyes and recommends it as part of a daily nourishing diet (Ashtanga Hridaya, Sutrasthana). Reducing coffee, alcohol, very spicy food, and excessive screen time late at night all help keep Vata and Pitta from aggravating the netra.

when to see a doctor or vaidya

The suggestions above are general supportive habits grounded in Ayurvedic tradition. They are not a diagnosis and are not a substitute for professional medical care. If your dry or watering eyes are severe, persistent, or getting worse despite simple care, please consult a qualified ophthalmologist or a registered vaidya. Seek prompt medical attention if you notice sudden changes in vision, significant pain inside the eye, redness that does not settle, discharge that is thick or yellow-green, sensitivity to light, or a sensation of something in the eye that washing does not resolve. These signs can indicate infections, glaucoma, dry eye disease, or other conditions that require proper clinical assessment and treatment.

Try This Today

  • Cooled triphala eye wash: Simmer half a teaspoon of triphala churna in two cups of water for five minutes. Cool completely, strain twice through fine cloth, and use the clear liquid to gently bathe each eye with a clean eye cup. Do this in the evening two or three times a week.
  • Rose water at end of day: Place one drop of pure, preservative-free rose water in each eye after a long stretch at a screen. It is cooling and soothing, especially when the eyes feel warm or irritated.
  • Oil the soles before sleep: Massage a small amount of warm sesame oil into the soles of your feet before bed. This is a traditional Vata-pacifying practice said to calm the nervous system and support the sense organs overnight.
  • Blink consciously at your screen: Set a reminder every twenty minutes to look away from your screen, blink ten times slowly, and let your gaze rest on something in the distance. This simple habit prevents the reflex drying that comes from sustained fixed staring.
  • Add a teaspoon of ghee daily: Stir a teaspoon of good-quality cow ghee into your dal, rice, or roti at one meal each day. The classical texts name ghee as among the most nourishing foods for eye tissue.
author: Kerala Ayurveda Editorial Team

metafields.dek: Dry, gritty, or watering eyes are more common than ever. Ayurveda has named and understood this condition for centuries, and its daily remedies are simple enough for anyone.
metafields.disclaimer: Ayurveda guidance on AyurHQ is supportive care, not emergency or diagnostic medical treatment.
at_a_glance: Dryness and grittiness are usually a sign of aggravated Vata in the eye tissues, often worsened by screens, late nights, and dry air.
Cooled triphala water used to bathe the eyes is the most classically recommended daily remedy for eye comfort.
Ghee in the diet, adequate sleep, and reducing screen time late at night are the simplest protective habits the classical texts recommend.

glossary_terms.definition: The eyes; the organ of sight, considered one of the five primary sense organs in Ayurveda.
The sub-dosha of Pitta that resides in the eyes and governs the capacity to perceive light and form.
The Ayurvedic daily routine: a set of morning and evening habits designed to maintain health and prevent disease.
Eye disease; Sushruta Samhita classifies dozens of eye conditions under this heading.
A classical formulation of three fruits (amalaki, bibhitaki, haritaki) used widely in Ayurveda for cleansing and nourishment.
The subtle luminous quality of the fire element; associated with Pitta and with the capacity of the eyes to perceive.

sources: Ashtanga Hridaya, Uttarasthana (eye health and netra tarpana)

faqs.question: What is the meaning of "Netra" in Ayurveda?
faqs.answer: In the Ayurvedic tradition, Netra is defined as: The eyes; the organ of sight, considered one of the five primary sense organs in Ayurveda.

faqs.question: How can I start addressing this issue today?
faqs.answer: One of the most recommended practices is to cooled triphala eye wash. Simmer half a teaspoon of triphala churna in two cups of water for five minutes. Cool completely, strain twice through fine cloth, and use the clear liquid to gently bathe each eye with a clean eye cup. Do this in the evening two or three times a week.

faqs.question: Are there any specific lifestyle habits I should adopt?
faqs.answer: A key suggestion is to rose water at end of day. Place one drop of pure, preservative-free rose water in each eye after a long stretch at a screen. It is cooling and soothing, especially when the eyes feel warm or irritated.
